title: The Angelus
summary: The thrice-daily prayer honoring the Incarnation.
history: The Angelus developed from the medieval practice of ringing church bells at set hours to call the faithful to prayer. By the 14th century, the three daily Angelus bells (morning, noon, evening) were established throughout Christendom. The prayer commemorates the Annunciation — the moment the Word was made flesh. Pope Benedict XIV (1742) granted indulgences for its recitation. Jean-François Millet's painting 'The Angelus' (1857) depicts peasants pausing in the fields to pray at the sound of the bells.
explanation: The Angelus consists of three versicles and responses recounting the Annunciation, each followed by a Hail Mary, and concluding with a collect prayer. The central verse — 'Et Verbum caro factum est / And the Word was made flesh' — is the climax, at which a genuflection is made. During Eastertide (Easter Sunday through the Saturday after Pentecost), the Regina Cæli replaces the Angelus.
practice: Pray the Angelus three times daily: at 6 AM, noon, and 6 PM (or when you hear church bells). Stand for the prayer, genuflecting at 'Et Verbum caro factum est.' During Eastertide, pray the Regina Cæli instead, standing throughout (as is proper during the Easter season).

title: The Divine Office
summary: The official prayer of the Church, sanctifying every hour of the day.
history: The Divine Office (also called the Liturgy of the Hours or Breviary) has its roots in Jewish prayer practice — the Psalms prayed at set hours — adopted and developed by the early Church. By the 6th century, St. Benedict codified the monastic hours in his Rule. The Roman Breviary was standardized after the Council of Trent by Pope St. Pius V in 1568. The traditional Office consists of eight canonical hours prayed throughout the day and night.
explanation: The eight hours are: Matins (during the night or early morning), Lauds (dawn), Prime (first hour, ~6 AM), Terce (third hour, ~9 AM), Sext (sixth hour, noon), None (ninth hour, ~3 PM), Vespers (evening), and Compline (before sleep). The Office consists primarily of Psalms, hymns, antiphons, scripture readings, and prayers. Priests and religious are obligated to pray the Office daily; the laity are encouraged to join in this prayer, especially Lauds, Vespers, and Compline.
practice: For laypeople beginning the Office, start with Compline (Night Prayer) — it is short, beautiful, and relatively simple. Vespers is the next most accessible hour. A traditional Breviary or the Diurnal (daytime hours only) is needed. The 1962 Breviary corresponds to the 1962 Missal.
churchTeaching: Second Vatican Council, Sacrosanctum Concilium §100: 'Pastors of souls should see to it that the chief hours, especially Vespers, are celebrated in common in church on Sundays and the more solemn feasts.'

title: First Friday Devotion
summary: Nine consecutive First Fridays in honor of the Sacred Heart.
history: The First Friday devotion originates from the private revelations of Our Lord to St. Margaret Mary Alacoque (1647-1690), a Visitandine nun in Paray-le-Monial, France. In these apparitions, Jesus revealed His Sacred Heart and made twelve promises to those devoted to it. The ninth promise — the 'Great Promise' — assures the grace of final perseverance to those who receive Communion on nine consecutive First Fridays. These revelations were approved by the Church, and Pope Clement XIII approved the feast of the Sacred Heart in 1765.
explanation: The Great Promise: 'I promise thee, in the excessive mercy of My Heart, that My all-powerful love will grant to all those who receive Holy Communion on the First Friday of nine consecutive months the grace of final perseverance; they shall not die in My disgrace, nor without receiving their sacraments. My Divine Heart shall be their safe refuge in that last moment.' This is understood as a promise of the grace of final repentance, not a guarantee regardless of one's state of life.
practice: On nine consecutive First Fridays: attend Mass, receive Holy Communion in the state of grace (go to Confession if needed), and offer the Communion in reparation to the Sacred Heart. If a month is missed, the nine must be restarted. Many parishes offer First Friday Mass and devotions specifically for this intention.
churchTeaching: Pope Pius XI, Miserentissimus Redemptor (1928), promoted devotion to the Sacred Heart and acts of reparation.

title: First Saturday Devotion
summary: Five consecutive First Saturdays in reparation to the Immaculate Heart.
history: On December 10, 1925, Our Lady appeared to Sr. Lucia of Fatima in Pontevedra, Spain, holding the Child Jesus. She showed her heart encircled by thorns and said: 'Look, my daughter, at my Heart, surrounded with thorns with which ungrateful men pierce me at every moment by their blasphemies and ingratitude. You at least try to console me and announce in my name that I promise to assist at the hour of death, with all the graces necessary for salvation, all those who, on the first Saturday of five consecutive months, shall confess, receive Holy Communion, recite five decades of the Rosary, and keep me company for fifteen minutes while meditating on the fifteen mysteries of the Rosary, with the intention of making reparation to me.'
explanation: The five blasphemies against the Immaculate Heart that require reparation are: blasphemies against her Immaculate Conception, against her Virginity, against her Divine Maternity, blasphemies that seek to instill contempt for Our Lady in children's hearts, and offenses that directly outrage her sacred images. Our Lord told Sr. Lucia that Confession may be made within eight days of the First Saturday.
practice: On five consecutive First Saturdays: (1) Go to Confession (within 8 days before or after), (2) Receive Holy Communion, (3) Pray five decades of the Rosary, (4) Keep Our Lady company for 15 minutes meditating on the mysteries of the Rosary. All must be done with the intention of making reparation to the Immaculate Heart of Mary.
churchTeaching: The Fatima apparitions were approved by the Bishop of Leiria in 1930. Pope Pius XII consecrated the world to the Immaculate Heart of Mary in 1942.

title: Examination of Conscience
summary: The nightly review of the soul essential for spiritual growth.
history: The practice of examining one's conscience daily dates to the earliest days of the Church. The Stoic philosophers practiced a similar review, but the Christian form is oriented toward God's mercy and amendment of life. St. Ignatius of Loyola formalized the 'Examen' in his Spiritual Exercises (1548), making it a cornerstone of Ignatian spirituality. However, the practice is universal in Catholic tradition — St. John Chrysostom, St. Bernard, and St. Francis de Sales all strongly recommended it.
explanation: The traditional nightly examen consists of: (1) Place yourself in the presence of God, (2) Thank God for the graces of the day, (3) Review the day hour by hour, examining your thoughts, words, and deeds, (4) Express sorrow for your sins and failings, (5) Pray the Act of Contrition, (6) Resolve to amend tomorrow with God's grace. This practice is essential preparation for regular Confession.
practice: Perform the examen every night before sleep. It should take 5-15 minutes. Use the Ten Commandments or the seven deadly sins as a framework for examination. Keep a brief mental note of recurring sins to confess. This practice, maintained faithfully, is transformative for the spiritual life.
